title: The woman who waves at the train
summary: For thirty-one years Mrs Phuong has waved at the express train. She started for one person. Now she waves for everyone — and forty-two letters say she has been seen.
body:
Every morning at six twenty-three, the express train passes the bottom of Mrs Phuong's garden. The train does not stop. It is going from Hanoi to Da Nang.

For thirty-one years, Mrs Phuong has waved at the train. She started when her son first took it, to work in Da Nang. She wanted him to see her from his seat. She did not know if he saw her, but she waved anyway.

Her son returned home in 2008. He has not taken the train for sixteen years. Mrs Phuong is seventy-eight now. She still waves.

The passengers do not all see her. But some look out at her garden. They see an old woman in a white shirt, waving. Once or twice a year, a stranger writes her a letter. The letter says, 'I saw you. Thank you for waving.'

She keeps the letters in a small wooden box. There are forty-two letters. None are from her son.

She has been asked why she still waves. She gave different answers. To her granddaughter, she said, 'When I wave, I am not old. I am the woman who waves at the train.'

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect with 'for' — actions still ongoing
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng thì hiện tại hoàn thành (have/has + V3) với 'for + khoảng thời gian' để nói một việc bắt đầu trong quá khứ và còn tiếp diễn đến hiện tại. Trong bài: 'For thirty-one years, Mrs Phuong has waved at the train.' (Suốt ba mươi mốt năm, bà Phượng đã vẫy tay với đoàn tàu.) / 'He has not taken the train for sixteen years.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use the present perfect (have/has + past participle) with 'for + period' for an action that began in the past and continues now. From the story: 'For thirty-one years, Mrs Phuong has waved at the train.' / 'He has not taken the train for sixteen years.'

cloze.template:
Every morning at six twenty-three, the {{b1}} train passes the bottom of Mrs Phuong's garden. The train does not stop. It is going from Hanoi to Da Nang.

For {{b2}} years, Mrs Phuong has waved at the train. She started when her son first took it, to work in Da Nang. She wanted him to see her from his seat. She did not know if he saw her, but she waved {{b3}}.

Her son returned home in {{b4}}. He has not taken the train for {{b5}} years. Mrs Phuong is {{b6}} now. She still waves.

The passengers do not all see her. But some look out at her garden. They see an old woman in a {{b7}} shirt, waving. Once or twice a year, a {{b8}} writes her a letter. The letter says, 'I saw you. Thank you for waving.'

She keeps the letters in a small {{b9}} box. There are {{b10}} letters. None are from her son.

She has been asked why she still waves. She gave different answers. To her {{b11}}, she said, 'When I wave, I am not {{b12}}. I am the woman who waves at the train.'

blanks.explanation: The express train — a fast, non-stopping train.
She waved anyway — even though she did not know if her son saw her.
He has not taken the train for sixteen years.
An old woman in a white shirt — what passengers see.
A stranger writes her a letter — someone she has never met.
She keeps the letters in a small wooden box.
She said this to her granddaughter.
'When I wave, I am not old.' — the waving keeps her from being defined by age.

translationVi:
Mỗi sáng lúc sáu giờ hai mươi ba phút, tàu tốc hành đi qua phía cuối vườn nhà bà Phượng. Tàu không dừng. Tàu đi từ Hà Nội vào Đà Nẵng.

Suốt ba mươi mốt năm, bà Phượng đã vẫy tay với đoàn tàu. Bà bắt đầu khi con trai bà lần đầu lên tàu để vào Đà Nẵng làm việc. Bà muốn anh thấy bà từ ghế ngồi. Bà không biết anh có thấy bà không, nhưng bà vẫn vẫy.

Con trai bà về hẳn nhà năm 2008. Anh đã không đi chuyến tàu đó suốt mười sáu năm. Bà Phượng giờ bảy mươi tám tuổi. Bà vẫn vẫy.

Không phải hành khách nào cũng thấy bà. Nhưng một số nhìn ra phía vườn nhà bà. Họ thấy một bà cụ mặc áo trắng, đang vẫy tay. Mỗi năm một hai lần, có một người lạ viết thư cho bà. Thư nói: 'Cháu đã thấy bà. Cảm ơn bà đã vẫy tay.'

Bà cất thư trong một chiếc hộp gỗ nhỏ. Có bốn mươi hai lá thư. Không lá nào của con trai bà.

Đã có người hỏi bà sao bà vẫn vẫy. Bà cho những câu trả lời khác nhau. Với cháu gái bà, bà nói: 'Khi bà vẫy, bà không già. Bà là người phụ nữ vẫy tay với đoàn tàu.'

questions.prompt: What time does the train pass Mrs Phuong's garden?
options: At six twenty-three in the morning
From Da Nang to Hai Phong
questions.explanation: 'It is going from Hanoi to Da Nang.'

questions.prompt: How long has Mrs Phuong been waving at the train?
questions.explanation: 'For thirty-one years, Mrs Phuong has waved at the train.'

options: Her son first took the train to work in Da Nang and she wanted him to see her from his seat
questions.explanation: 'She started when her son first took it, to work in Da Nang. She wanted him to see her from his seat.'

questions.prompt: When did her son return home?
How old is Mrs Phuong now?
What do passengers who look out see?
options: An old woman in a white shirt, waving
questions.explanation: 'They see an old woman in a white shirt, waving.'

questions.prompt: How many letters has she received from passengers?
Who are NONE of the letters from?
What does Mrs Phuong say to her granddaughter?
options: 'When I wave, I am not old. I am the woman who waves at the train.'
questions.explanation: Her quoted answer to her granddaughter.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Mrs Phuong started waving for one person but now waves for everyone — and forty-two strangers have written to thank her. Think of a small habit you do that started for one reason. Has its reason quietly changed over time? What is it really for now?
